props.topic: HUMANITARIANS AI · Build a Multiplicity Peak Bar Chart with Claude Code + Manim

beats.narration_text: Humanitarians AI — Claude Code, Manim, and the physics, taken apart.

props.command: Build a Multiplicity Peak Bar Chart with Claude Code + Manim
props.segment: Build a Multiplicity Peak Bar Chart with Claude Code + Manim

beats.narration_text: Here is why Build a Multiplicity Peak Bar Chart with Claude Code + Manim belongs in your working knowledge. The systems, policies, and tools shaped by it affect decisions at your level — whether or not you understand the mechanism. Knowing the underlying logic gives you the frame; missing it means reasoning from output with no access to the inputs.

props.command: Build a Multiplicity Peak Bar Chart with Claude Code + Manim
props.segment: Build a Multiplicity Peak Bar Chart with Claude Code + Manim

beats.narration_text: The prompt. Claude Code terminal: claude — write a Manim scene, multiplicity bar chart. N equals ten spins. Plot Omega of N-comma-n equals N-choose-n for n from zero to ten. Mark the peak at n equals five with C of ten-comma-five equals two-fifty-two. Three things: the rule, the numbers, what to render.

props.command:
claude "write a Manim scene:
  multiplicity Ω(N,n) = C(N,n)
  N=10: bar chart for n=0..10
  peak at n=5: C(10,5)=252
  label peak height and n=N/2"

beats.narration_text: The CLI writes the file. Before running — read it. Find this line: from math import comb. Then Omega equals comb of N-comma-n. That is the entire statistical mechanics of an N-spin system. Check: comb of ten-comma-five equals two-fifty-two. And comb of ten-comma-four equals two-ten — sixteen percent lower than the peak. The distribution is already concentrated. Thirty seconds.

props.code:
# multiplicity.py  —  Claude Code output
from math import comb
import numpy as np

N = 10
n_vals = list(range(N+1))  # 0 to 10

# ← the physics: multiplicity (binomial coefficient)
Omega = [comb(N, n) for n in n_vals]

# Peak at n = N/2 = 5:
print(f'Omega(10,5) = {comb(10,5)}')  # 252
print(f'Omega(10,4) = {comb(10,4)}')  # 210

# Pascal symmetry: comb(N,n) == comb(N,N-n)
props.title: multiplicity.py

beats.narration_text: Run it. Two things to verify. First: the peak is at n equals five — exactly N over two. The bar at n-equals-five reaches two-fifty-two. Pascal's triangle has this symmetry for any N. Second: the distribution is already noticeably concentrated. The peak bar is twenty percent higher than the adjacent bars. Both checks pass. Ready to iterate.

Iterate: claude — update multiplicity dot py — change N to fifty. Watch the peak height explode and the distribution become dramatically sharper. The peak at n equals twenty-five dominates everything around it. This is why equilibrium is stable: the vast majority of microstates live near n equals N over two. One parameter, one revelation, one re-run.

props.command:
claude "update multiplicity.py:
  → change N from 10 to 50
  → normalize bars so peak=1 (log scale?)
  → show peak sharpens dramatically"
